1: wayhî kaʾašer šāmaʿ sanballat kî-ʾănaḥnû bônîm ʾet-hahômāh wayyiḥar lô: 'and it came about that when Sanballat heard that we were building the wall, he was angry.' wayyiḥar lô is the idiom for anger — literally 'it burned for him' (ḥārāh = to burn, be angry). The anger vocabulary intensifies: kāʿas gāḏôl (great vexation, provocation) in v.1 — kāʿas is the emotional disturbance of someone who sees their position threatened. Sanballat's reaction is public and deliberate: yᵉdabbēr lip̄nê ʾăḥāyw ûḇêl samaryîn, 'he spoke before his brothers and the army of Samaria' — the mockery is addressed to the Samaritan military establishment, intended to delegitimize the project politically before it gains momentum.
2: wayyōʾmer mah-hāyᵉhûḏîm hāʾumᵉlālîm ʿōśîm: 'and he said, what are these feeble Jews doing?' ʾumᵉlāl (feeble, powerless) comes from ʾāmal, to languish or be enfeebled — a term of contempt. The series of rhetorical questions parses the impossibility of the project: (1) Will they fortify themselves? (2) Will they sacrifice? (3) Will they finish in a day? (4) Will they revive the stones from the heaps of rubble? The fourth question is particularly pointed: hayᵉḥayyû ʾet-hāʾăḇānîm mēʿarᵉmôt hāʿāp̄ār, 'will they revive the stones from the piles of ash/dust?' The rubble was not usable building material — it had been burned and shattered. Sanballat's questions may reflect genuine strategic assessment: the project was technically improbable.
3: wᵉṭôḇiyyāh hāʿammōnî ʾeṣlô wayyōʾmer gam ʾašer-hēm bônîm ʾim-yaʿăleh šûʿāl ûp̄āraṣ ḥômat ʾaḇnêhem: 'and Tobiah the Ammonite was beside him and said, even what they are building — if a fox goes up, it would breach their stone wall.' The fox simile (šûʿāl = fox or jackal) is a merciless attack on the quality of the construction: the walls are so poorly built that even the weight of a small animal would collapse them. ûp̄āraṣ (would breach) — the verb used of a military breach; the irony is a wild animal causing a military-grade collapse. The dismissal was both tactical (demoralization) and possibly accurate: rubble-built walls without proper mortar or foundations would be structurally weak.
4: šᵉmaʿ ʾĕlōhênû kî-hāyînû ḇûzāh: 'hear, our God, for we have become an object of contempt.' Nehemiah's imprecatory prayer (vv.4-5) is the most direct of his four prayers in the memoir. ḥerpātām (their reproach) — the same ḥerpāh term from 1:3 and 2:17 now inverted: God should turn their mockery back on their own heads. wᵉhāšēḇ ḥerpātām ʾel-rōʾšām: 'and return their taunt upon their own head.' The prayer calls for covenant retribution: those who shame the covenant people should experience the shame they inflicted. The logic is Deuteronomic — the mockers are attacking YHWH's project, and YHWH's honor is implicated in the wall's outcome.
5: wᵉʾal-tᵉḵas ʿal-ʿăwōnām: 'and do not cover their iniquity.' kāsāh (to cover) is the verb for atonement in some contexts — here Nehemiah prays for the opposite: uncovering, not covering. wᵉḥaṭṭāʾtām mipᵉneḵā ʾal-timmāḥeh: 'and do not let their sin be blotted out from before you.' The prayer echoes Moses' anti-intercession for those who abandon the covenant (cf. Ps 69:28, 'let them be blotted out of the book of the living'). The justification: kî hirkîʾsûnî linegeḏ habbōnîm, 'for they have provoked you to anger before the builders' — the mockery was not private but public, calculated to demoralize, and therefore a public affront to YHWH.
6: wanniḇneh ʾet-hahômāh wattiqṣar kol-hahômāh ʿad-haḥᵉṣyāh: 'so we built the wall, and the entire wall was joined together to half its height.' Despite the mockery, the work continued: wayyᵉḥî lēḇ lāʿām laʿăśôt, 'for the people had a mind/heart to work.' lēḇ (heart/mind) — the interior will to act — is what sustained the project through external opposition. The half-height completion is a significant milestone: the wall now existed as a continuous circuit, even if not full-height. The phrase kol-hahômāh (the entire wall) emphasizes completeness of the circuit before full height was achieved.
7: wayhî kaʾašer šāmaʿ sanballat wᵉṭôḇiyyāh wᵉhāʿărāḇîm wᵉhāʿammônîm wᵉhāʾašdōdîm: 'and it came about that when Sanballat, Tobiah, the Arabs, the Ammonites, and the Ashdodites heard.' The opposition broadens beyond the original three to include the people of Ashdod — the old Philistine city to the west. All four geographic directions are now represented: north (Samaria/Sanballat), east (Ammon/Tobiah), south (Arabia/Geshem), west (Ashdod). kî-ʿālᵉtāh ʾărûḵāh lᵉḥōmôt yᵉrûšālaim: 'that the healing of the walls of Jerusalem was advancing' — ʾārûḵāh (healing, restoration) applied to walls is an unusual metaphor; walls can be 'healed' as a wound heals, closing the breach.
8: wayyiqqāšᵉrû kullām yaḥdāw lāḇôʾ lᵉhillāḥēm bîrûšālaim wᵉlaʿăśôt-lô tôʿāh: 'and they conspired together to come and attack Jerusalem and cause confusion in it.' qāšar (to conspire, bind together) signals a coalition plot. Two objectives: lᵉhillāḥēm (to fight) and laʿăśôt-lô tôʿāh (to create confusion/disorder). Tôʿāh (confusion, error, disturbance) is the strategy of sowing disorganization within the workforce — not merely a military attack but a psychological campaign to disrupt the project's coordination.
9: wannitpallēl ʾel-ʾĕlōhênû wannaʿămîḏ mišmār ʿălêhem yômām wālaylāh mippᵉnêhem: 'and we prayed to our God and posted a guard against them day and night because of them.' The two responses are parallel and simultaneous: prayer (hitpallēl) and practical defense (mišmār = guard duty). Nehemiah does not contrast prayer and action as alternatives — they are paired. The prayer is not instead of the guard; the guard is not instead of prayer. Mišmār (guard, watch) is both noun and concept; its day-and-night character means continuous vigilance, no gaps. The Ephesians 6:18 parallel ('praying at all times…with all perseverance, making supplication for all the saints') captures exactly this dual posture.
10: wayyōʾmer yᵉhûḏāh kāšal kōaḥ hansabbāl wᵉhāʿāp̄ār harbēh wᵉʾănaḥnû lōʾ nûḵal liḇnôt baḥômāh: 'and Judah said, the strength of the laborers is failing and there is much rubble, and we ourselves cannot build the wall.' This verse represents the internal discouragement within the workforce — a complaint from the builders of Judah themselves. Three problems: (1) physical exhaustion (kāšal kōaḥ = stumbling/failing strength), (2) overwhelming rubble (hāʿāp̄ār harbēh = much dust/debris), (3) self-doubt (lōʾ nûḵal = we are not able). The laborers' complaint is realistic; the project had now continued long enough for fatigue to set in alongside external opposition.
11: wayyōʾmᵉrû ṣārênû lōʾ yēḏᵉʿû wᵉlōʾ yirʾû ʿad ʾašer-nāḇôʾ ʾel-tôḵām: 'and our enemies said, they will not know or see anything until we come among them and kill them and stop the work.' The enemies' strategy is a covert attack — enter undetected (bôʾ ʾel-tôḵām = come into their midst) and kill the workers before reinforcements can arrive. wahăšiḇônû ʾet-hamᵉlāʾḵāh: 'and cause the work to cease' — the ultimate goal is not territorial conquest but project termination. The psychological pressure is compounded by proximity: the workers' enemies were close enough to communicate their plans to allies inside Jerusalem.
12: wayhî kaʾašer bāʾû hayyᵉhûḏîm hayōšᵉḇîm ʾeṣlām wayyōʾmᵉrû lānû ʿeśer pᵉʿāmîm: 'and it came about that the Jews who lived near them came and said to us ten times.' ʿeśer pᵉʿāmîm (ten times) is an idiom for repeated insistence (cf. Gen 31:7; Num 14:22; Job 19:3) — the warning was persistent and urgent. mikkol-hamᵉqōmôt ʾašer-tāšûḇû ʿālênû: the reading is difficult — possibly 'from all the places you will return to us' indicating the peripheral workers who returned from their home areas brought warnings about enemy movements.
13: waʾaʿămîḏ mittaḥtiyyôt lammāqôm mēʾaḥărê lahômāh batsᵉḥîḥîm waʾaʿămîḏ ʾet-hāʿām lᵉmišpāḥôt: 'and I stationed people in the lower places behind the wall, in exposed places, and I stationed the people by their families.' The response is tactical: (1) strategic positioning at vulnerable points in the wall circuit; (2) organization by family units (mišpāḥôt) rather than by trade or district. Family cohesion as a military organizing principle was standard in ancient warfare — men fight harder for their families beside them. ḥarbôtām rōmᵉḥêhem wᵉqaštôtām: swords, spears, and bows — a fully equipped defense force.
14: wāʾeʾreh wāʾāqûm wāʾōmar ʾel-haḥōrîm wᵉʾel-hassᵉgānîm wᵉʾel-yeter hāʿām: 'and I looked and rose and said to the nobles and the officials and the rest of the people.' Nehemiah's speech follows his tactical deployment. The address to all social levels — nobles, officials, people — reflects his characteristic unified-front approach. The exhortation: ʾal-tîrᵉʾû mippᵉnêhem, 'do not be afraid of them.' Then the motivating theology: ʾet-YHWH haggāḏôl wᵉhannôrāʾ zikrû, 'remember the great and awesome LORD.' The covenant formula gāḏôl wᵉnôrāʾ (great and awesome) from Nehemiah's own prayer (1:5) is now deployed as an exhortation to the workers. wᵉhillaḥămû ʾal-ʾăḥêkem bᵉnêkem ûḇᵉnōtêkem nᵉšêkem ûḇāttêkem: fight for brothers, sons, daughters, wives, and houses — the appeal is to family and home, the most primal motivations.
15: wayhî kaʾašer šāmᵉʿû ʾôyᵉḇênû kî-nôḏaʿ lānû wayyāp̄er hāʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʿăṣātām: 'and it came about that when our enemies heard that their plan was known to us, God frustrated their counsel.' nôḏaʿ lānû: 'it was known to us' — the intelligence leak or God's own disclosure disrupted the covert attack plan. wayyāp̄er hāʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʿăṣātām: 'God frustrated their counsel' — pārar (to break, frustrate, annul) is the verb used for God breaking human plans (Ps 33:10; Prov 21:30). Nehemiah attributes the intelligence breakthrough directly to God. wannāšāḇ kullānû ʾel-hahômāh: 'and we all returned to the wall, each to his own work.' The return to work is itself an act of faith: the intelligence has been received, the threat assessed, God acknowledged — now back to building.
16: wayhî min-hayyôm hahûʾ ḥᵉṣî naʿărāy ʿōśîm bamᵉlāʾḵāh wᵉḥᵉṣyām maḥăzîqîm baḥănîtîm: 'and from that day on, half of my servants worked on the construction, and half of them held the spears.' The dual workforce — half building, half defending — is Nehemiah's tactical adaptation to continuous threat. wᵉhassinnôt wᵉhaqšātôt wᵉhašširyônôt wᵉhappᵉnîgîm: 'the shields, bows, coats of mail, and commanders' — the full defensive apparatus. Śārîm (commanders) in v.16 are the officers who supervised the armed half while Nehemiah and the rebuilders directed the construction half.
17: hammāśôr babbinyān kāl-ʾeḥāḏ bᵉʾaḥat yāḏô ʿōśeh bamᵉlāʾḵāh wᵉʾaḥat maḥăzéqet haššālaḥ: 'those who built the wall, each with one hand laboring on the work and with the other holding a weapon.' The image of the one-handed builder is one of the most vivid in the Hebrew Bible — a man simultaneously building and defending, unable to devote full attention to either but committed to both. Hašālaḥ (weapon, javelin) held in the defending hand while the building hand works. The verse has become an emblem of engaged ministry under pressure: the work continues despite threat; the threat does not stop the work.
18: wᵉhabbōnîm ʾîš ḥarbô ʾăzûrāh ʿal-motnāyw ûḇônîm: 'and the builders, each with his sword girded at his side, and they built.' The sword (ḥereb) is ʾăzûrāh ʿal-motnāyw, 'bound at his loins' — the battle posture. Building and sword-wearing simultaneously expresses the paradox of the whole chapter. wᵉhaššôpēr ʾeṣlî: 'and the trumpeter was beside me' — Nehemiah himself had a dedicated trumpeter at his side. The šôpār (ram's horn trumpet) was the standard ancient Israelite signal instrument for military alerts (cf. Judg 3:27; 6:34; 1 Sam 13:3). Its proximity to Nehemiah meant the rally signal was always under his personal control.
19: wāʾōmar ʾel-haḥōrîm wᵉʾel-hassᵉgānîm wᵉʾel yeter hāʿām hammᵉlāʾḵāh rabbāh ûgᵉḏôlāh: 'and I said to the nobles, the officials, and the rest of the people, the work is great and extensive.' The logistical challenge is acknowledged directly: the wall circuit was long, the workers spread out, the sections separated from each other. waʾănaḥnû nip̄rāḏîm ʿal-hahômāh: 'and we are separated from one another on the wall.' The dispersal of the workforce created communication gaps that made coordinated defense difficult — the trumpet system addressed exactly this problem.
20: bammāqôm ʾašer tišmᵉʿû ʾet-qôl haššôp̄ār šāmmāh tiqqābᵉṣû ʾēlênû: 'wherever you hear the sound of the trumpet, rally to us there.' The trumpet as a communication system: one blast = gather to that point. ʾĕlōhênû yillāḥem lānû: 'our God will fight for us.' This is the holy-war formula distilled to its essence — five Hebrew words expressing the Deuteronomic conviction that YHWH fights Israel's battles (Deut 20:4; Exod 14:14). The formula transforms the defensive coordination from a merely military maneuver into a theological declaration: the defense of Jerusalem is YHWH's battle, and the workers are his instruments.
21: waʾănaḥnû ʿōśîm bamᵉlāʾḵāh waḥᵉṣyām maḥăzîqîm baḥănîtîm mēʿălôt haššaḥar ʿad ṣēʾt hakkôḵāḇîm: 'and we did the work, with half holding the spears from the rise of dawn until the stars came out.' The working day is measured astronomically: from the first light (ʿălôt haššaḥar = the rising of the dawn) to the appearing of the stars (ṣēʾt hakkôḵāḇîm) — sunrise to full darkness, a maximum labor day. The extended hours reflect both the urgency of the project and the intensity of the threat: there was no safe time to reduce the guard.
22: gam bāʿēt hahîʾ āmartî lāʿām ʾîš wᵉnaʿărô yālînû bᵉṯôḵ yᵉrûšālaim: 'also at that time I said to the people, let each man and his servant stay inside Jerusalem as a guard.' yālîn (overnight, spend the night) — rather than returning to their villages in the evening (standard practice for day-workers from surrounding towns), the workers were now to remain in Jerusalem overnight. This served two purposes: (1) the city was guarded through the night by the workforce, not just the permanent residents; (2) the workers were available for early morning resumption of work without travel time.
23: wᵉʾanî waʾaḥay waʾănāšay wᵉhaššōmᵉrîm ʾašer ʾaḥărāy ʾên-ʾănaḥnû p̄ōšᵉṭîm ḇᵉgāḏênû: 'and I and my brothers and my servants and the men of the guard behind me — none of us removed our clothes.' The memoir concludes with the most personal detail: Nehemiah himself did not undress, even for sleep. ʾîš šilḥô hammāyim: the last phrase is textually difficult — possibly 'each had his weapon in his right hand' or 'each sent for water' (keeping his weapon even while fetching water). The final image is of relentless vigilance: the leader who set the tone of constant readiness lived it himself, clothes on, weapon in hand, maintaining the posture of alert service from first light to last star.
